epigraph.text:
Divide your portion to seven, or even to eight, for 
you do not know what misfortune may occur.

content.text:
Just as integers can be divided to get a quotient and remainder, 
polynomials can be divided similarly. This technique is essential for 
factoring higher-degree polynomials and finding zeros.

content.title: Polynomial Long Division
content.content:
1. Arrange both polynomials in descending order (include zero 
 coefficients for missing terms)

2. Divide the leading term of the dividend by the leading term of 
 the divisor

3. Multiply the divisor by this result and subtract

4. Bring down the next term and repeat

5. Continue until the remainder's degree is less than the divisor's

content.title: Basic long division
content.solution:
\[
\begin{array}{r}
x^2 + 4x + 3 \\
x - 2 \,\overline{\big)\, x^3 + 2x^2 - 5x - 6} \\
\underline{x^3 - 2x^2} \\
4x^2 - 5x \\
\underline{4x^2 - 8x} \\
3x - 6 \\
\underline{3x - 6} \\
0
\end{array}
\]

Quotient: $x^2 + 4x + 3$, Remainder: $0$

Since the remainder is 0, $(x - 2)$ is a factor:
\[
 x^3 + 2x^2 - 5x - 6 = (x - 2)(x^2 + 4x + 3)
\]

content.title: Division with missing terms
content.solution:
Rewrite with missing terms: $x^4 + 0x^3 + 0x^2 + 0x - 1$

\[
\begin{array}{r}
x^3 - x^2 + x - 1 \\
x + 1 \,\overline{\big)\, x^4 + 0x^3 + 0x^2 + 0x - 1} \\
\underline{x^4 + x^3} \\
-x^3 + 0x^2 \\
\underline{-x^3 - x^2} \\
x^2 + 0x \\
\underline{x^2 + x} \\
-x - 1 \\
\underline{-x - 1} \\
0
\end{array}
\]

Result: $x^4 - 1 = (x + 1)(x^3 - x^2 + x - 1)$

content.text:
When dividing by a linear factor $(x - c)$, synthetic division provides 
a faster method.

content.title: Synthetic Division
content.content:
To divide $f(x)$ by $(x - c)$:

1. Write $c$ and the coefficients of $f(x)$ (include zeros for 
 missing terms)

2. Bring down the first coefficient

3. Multiply by $c$, add to the next coefficient

4. Repeat until finished

5. The last number is the remainder; others are quotient coefficients

content.title: Synthetic division with $x + 2$
content.solution:
Since we divide by $(x + 2) = (x - (-2))$, use $c = -2$:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
-2 & 1 & 5 & -2 & -24 \\
 & & -2 & -6 & 16 \\
\hline
 & 1 & 3 & -8 & -8
\end{array}
\]

Quotient: $x^2 + 3x - 8$, Remainder: $-8$

content.title: Verifying a zero
content.problem: Verify that $x = 2$ is a zero of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6$.
content.solution:
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
2 & 1 & -6 & 11 & -6 \\
 & & 2 & -8 & 6 \\
\hline
 & 1 & -4 & 3 & 0
\end{array}
\]

The remainder is 0, confirming $x = 2$ is a zero.

Factorization: $f(x) = (x - 2)(x^2 - 4x + 3) = (x - 2)(x - 1)(x - 3)$

content.title: Remainder Theorem
content.content:
When a polynomial $f(x)$ is divided by $(x - c)$, the remainder equals 
$f(c)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This powerful theorem lets us evaluate polynomials using synthetic 
division.
The Factor Theorem is a direct consequence of the Remainder Theorem:

content.title: Factor Theorem
content.content: $(x - c)$ is a factor of $f(x)$ if and only if $f(c) = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Finding all zeros using division
content.problem:
Find all zeros of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 11x + 12$ given that $x = 4$ is 
a zero.

marginNotes.text:
Who then is the faithful and wise manager, 
whom the master puts in charge of his servants?
